title: Cru World Wine sees huge demand for its ‘community-ownership’ share offer
summary:
Cru World Wine has launched a new initiative to become partially ‘community-owned’, as it plans to become the “Right Move of fine wine”.

The post Cru World Wine sees huge demand for its ‘community-ownership’ share offer appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Cru World Wine has launched a new offer to its clients to become partially ‘community-owned’, as it plans to become the “Right Move of fine wine”. The company is offering the users of its platform the chance to become full voting shareholder in the company.  As well as holding a financial stake, they will receive a range of benefits and rewards including loyalty points (called Vinios) on their purchases and storage, specially selected offers and rare parcels, priority on allocations, lower selling fees, a £250,000 bidding limit, free delivery on most orders, and the chance to borrow up to 65% of their value of their portfolios at preferential rates. According to chief executive Jeremy Howard, it is not only a way for the company to differentiate itself from its competitors, but also to give clients a sense of partnership and a stake in the value that they help create. Fine wine collectors currently receive little or no benefit from transacting with a fine wine merchant, he pointed out, despite funding the merchant's operating costs and the the team are looking to redefine this relationship. It is he a model that no one has really tried to apply to fine wine - although he recognised the success of the Wine Society’s mutual organisation. “We wanted to create more of an alignment where clients are incentivised to do more of their business with us and store more wine with us,” he explained to the drinks business in an exclusive interview. “There are people who are spending a lot of money storing a lot of wine and creating quite a lot of value for merchants, but in the traditional model, they don't get any benefit from that.” The bigger the company gets, in theory, the more valuable their shareholding will be. “Marketplaces thrive on network effects: the more buyers and sellers, the greater the liquidity for everyone. As transaction volumes increase, services such as storage become more efficient, reducing costs across the platform,” he said. “We're not looking to sort of take every last ounce of margin out for our owners, but see [customers] as long-term partners.” There is also a strong element of it instilling a greater sense of loyalty among its client-base, which can be difficult, for fine wine collectors and investor tend to talk to, and buy from, lots of different companies, and potentially store it somewhere else. “Engendering that loyalty is really quite difficult,” he said. “Rather than giving away points or incentives and free days here or trips to that, maybe we should go the whole hog and try to create a tier of owners who actually have a real stake in the company.”

Spun out

The company was originally spun out of Bibendum’s fine wine business back in 2013 by Simon Farr, who became Cru World Wine’s chairman, and Jeremy Howard, chief executive, with the backing investment of a European family office, who owned around 60% of the business. The aim is to raise £1.5 million, which translates at around 7% of the business, “a nice sort of sizeable minority,” Howard notes, but interest has already far exceeded this, even before the offer goes full live. “We had a very significant response to our early access push, so we’ve had just over 420 clients now who indicated that they'd like to invest and become owners, and that a very substantial amount!” he said. This came as something of a surprise, given that the fine wine is a traditional market and “you never quite know what the response is going to be.” As well as growing the company, the team are also looking at providing exit possibilities so that at a later day people will be able to buy and sell share to realise some liquidity. “It would be nice to give them the chance to realize some value rather than be [permanently] locked in,” he said. In the meantime, the  investment boost will be used to harness new technology to improve the experience of fine wine collectors, for as Howard points out, Cru World Wide is “all about being a technologically advanced platform and using AI and all kinds of other things”.

Critical time

Howard argues that fine wine is entering a period “of profound structural change”, as there are” too many sub-scale merchants and fragmented inventory holders within the distribution chain creating duplicated costs and unnecessary friction.” A lot of fine wine companies have cost bases that are sized for Covid levels of business, he explained, which masked the inefficiencies, but since the slump of 2022, many of the companies are really struggling and consolidation may be inevitable. “We're hoping to formalize a process whereby we effectively act as the selling agent and operations clearance for other merchants and allow them to get back to what they really want, which is buying wines and talking directly to their own clients,” he said.

title: ‘Fantastic time to be a whisky drinker’ as Hedonism launches own-label range
summary:
Mayfair drinks boutique Hedonism Wines may be best-known for its well-heeled clientele and high-priced bottles, but the retailer’s new own-label whiskies aim to combine exclusivity with affordability – aided, ironically enough, by difficult trading conditions in the whisky market. Richard Woodard reports.

The post ‘Fantastic time to be a whisky drinker’ as Hedonism launches own-label range appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content: Mayfair drinks boutique Hedonism Wines may be best-known for its well-heeled clientele and high-priced bottles, but the retailer’s new own-label whiskies aim to combine exclusivity with affordability – aided, ironically enough, by difficult trading conditions in the whisky market. Richard Woodard reports. The recent downturn in the rare whisky category has been difficult to ignore, consumers voting with their wallets as prices reached unsustainable levels, exacerbated by deteriorating macroeconomic conditions. But now there are signs of a recalibration in the sector and, as pricing softens, might people be drawn back into the market? It can be hard to measure this kind of shift but, even at the very top of the market, launches appear to have been priced more realistically this year. https://www.thedrinksbusiness.com/2026/05/quality-over-quantity-for-todays-rare-whisky-releases/ Now upscale London drinks retailer Hedonism Wines has unveiled its first own-label single malt whiskies, and the chatter surrounding the launch centres more on affordability than luxury. The first Hedonism whiskies are a pair of single-cask, cask-strength single malts: a 20-year-old Laphroaig (52.3% ABV) at £195 a bottle, and a 15-year-old Bunnahabhain (50.5% ABV) at £115. The Laphroaig was filled into a refill ex-Bourbon cask on 21 September 2005 and bottled in April; the Bunna’ was distilled on 11 November 2010, matured in a first-fill ex-Sherry hogshead and bottled in February. The Laphroaig is said to show its trademark “maritime, medicinal and heavily peated” character, while the Bunnahabhain displays “rich, nutty Sherry notes” and the distillery’s “signature oily texture and coastal character”. In both cases, the quantities are small: only 128 bottles and 59 bottles respectively – and the casks are now empty. According to David Mellor, Hedonism’s head of spirits, the move into own-label independent bottling was a logical next step, given that the retailer has been collaborating with distilleries on exclusive products for more than a decade. He adds: “Whisky pricing has broadly moved down since the post-pandemic peak, and this has also affected casks. As bottlers and distilleries look to reduce their inventories, there’s an opportunity to acquire some very special casks. “More importantly, it’s about what our customers are asking for. What a certain type of client increasingly wants is the thing an independent bottling can offer that an official release often can’t: full transparency on cask type, distillation date, outturn and the specific reasoning why this cask was chosen.” Two single malts and fewer than 200 bottles constitutes a relatively low-key start to Hedonism’s own-label programme, but there’s more to come, including a “heavily Sherried” 19-year-old Glen Garioch, a 15-year-old Bowmore and a “highly limited” 50-year-old Scotch slated for release later this year. Beyond that, the liquid will have the final say. “We’re not putting pressure on ourselves to meet a release schedule or commit to a minimum number of bottlings per year – if we find something incredible, we’ll bottle it; if we don’t, we’ll keep searching,” Mellor explains. For now, Scotch is the sole focus, but he admits that single-barrel Bourbon would be “the logical next step”, adding: “We’ve secured some very interesting rum casks, which may make an appearance in 2027, but nothing concrete at this stage.” The overriding emphasis of the range, Hedonism says, is on “flavour, individuality and value, rather than collectability” – a signal, perhaps, of how the market has shifted after the fervour of the late 2010s and early 2020s. The thinking that underpins the choice of the whiskies, Mellor explains, is “a triangulation between distillery reputation, liquid quality and price point. We’re looking to bring great whiskies from well-loved distilleries to clients at a price that makes sense in the current market”. He cites the Laphroaig 20-year-old as a “prime example” of this philosophy. No doubt the central ethos is, at least in part, a response to changing market conditions – but those same conditions enable retailers like Hedonism to source quality casks with good age and provenance at more competitive prices in the first place. “There’s no denying that the market has fallen significantly from the heights of the post-pandemic boom, and it’s very much a buyer’s market in some respects – it’s part of a cycle we’ve seen repeated over the decades,” says Mellor. “We believe this is a fantastic time to be a whisky drinker … and a good time to be a bottler. Lots of fantastic casks are now looking for new homes as investors, blenders and distilleries rationalise their inventories. I expect we’ll see more delicious whiskies at wallet-friendly prices in the near future.”

title: Once again, Moutai bumps up price of signature spirit
summary:
For the second time this year, China’s largest liquor maker, Kweichow Moutai, has hiked the price of its flagship product in a bid to bolster margins amid a broader baijiu slump.

The post Once again, Moutai bumps up price of signature spirit appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
For the second time this year, China’s largest liquor maker, Kweichow Moutai, has hiked the price of its flagship product in a bid to bolster margins amid a broader baijiu slump. The baijiu giant has increased Feitian Moutai’s retail price on its official e-commerce website by 100 RMB to 1,639 RMB per bottle starting 18 July, according to an exchange filing.  The distiller also hiked the ex-factory price, charged to wholesalers and distributors, by 100 RMB to 1,369 RMB per 53% vol 500ml bottle. It’s the second time in the past few months that the company has upped both the ex-factor and retail price of the alcoholic drink – having previously done so in March. Prior to that, the price of the product had not lifted since 2023. The company’s shares lifted as much as 4.2% on Monday, as the spirits maker moved to safeguard profitability – hinting at an uptick in investor confidence. 

Getting over the hangover?

It’s a much-needed moment of positivity for China’s baijiu sector, which has been in decline for more than a year.  Analysts estimate that the whole baijiu sector sales fell by nearly 15% last year, with the giant producers such as Kweichow Maotai, Wuliangye Yibin, Luzhou Laojiao, Yanghe, and Xinghuacun all taking big hits, as previously reported by the drinks business. According to the ‘2025 Mid-term Research Report on China’s Liquor Market’ released by the China Alcoholic Drinks Association, in the first half of 2025, 58.1% of liquor companies reported increased channel inventory pressure, over half of distributors and retailers indicated an increase in the degree of price inversion, and more than 40% of retailers said they were facing cash flow pressures.

Looking back on a tough 2025

In the first three quarters of 2025, the total revenue of 20 A-share listed liquor companies amounted to Y317.79 billion, a year-on-year decrease of 5.90%; their combined net profit was Y122.571 billion, down 6.93%. Last year, Moutai suffered its first annual drop in profits and revenue since it listed on the Shanghai stock market in 2001. Net profit last year fell 4.5% to Y82.32 billion (US$12.07 billion) with revenue declining 1.2% to Y168.84 billion, both well below market expectations and short of the company’s own guidance.

title: Costco recalls plants potentially infested with grape-killing pests
summary:
California officials are urging Costco customers who recently bought desert willow plants to check them for signs of an invasive insect capable of spreading a disease that can kill grapevines and threaten the state's $73bn (£54bn) wine industry.

The post Costco recalls plants potentially infested with grape-killing pests appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
California officials are urging Costco customers who recently bought desert willow plants to check them for signs of an invasive insect capable of spreading a disease that can kill grapevines and threaten the state's $73bn (£54bn) wine industry. The warning applies to desert willow plants sold at Costco stores between 24 June and 3 July, after eggs from the glassy-winged sharpshooter were discovered on some of the plants. The insect is considered a major agricultural pest because it can spread Pierce's disease, a bacterial infection that is often fatal to grapevines. The glassy-winged sharpshooter, a large leafhopper native to the southeastern United States and northeastern Mexico, feeds on plant sap. As it moves between plants, it can transmit Xylella fastidiosa, the bacterium that causes Pierce's disease. According to the US Department of Agriculture (USDA), there is no cure for the disease. Once infected vines begin showing symptoms, they must be removed and destroyed to prevent the infection spreading further. Pierce's disease blocks a plant's water-conducting system, causing leaves to discolour, grapes to shrivel and vines to eventually die.

A threat to California's wine industry

The discovery marks the second time this year that the pest has been linked to plants sold through Costco. Earlier in 2026, glassy-winged sharpshooters were also found on grapevines sold by the retailer. Costco has written to affected customers, advising anyone who purchased a desert willow during the affected period to inspect the plant carefully and follow guidance from local agricultural authorities. Officials advise customers who have already planted the tree to try to isolate it and contact their county agricultural commissioner's office immediately for instructions. If the plant is to be disposed of, authorities advise placing it in two sealed bags before throwing it in the household rubbish. It should not be placed in compost, where the insect could spread further. California's wine industry, worth an estimated $73bn (£54bn) annually, is particularly vulnerable to Pierce's disease, making efforts to contain the glassy-winged sharpshooter a top priority for state agricultural officials.

title: Two Ukrainian wine professionals awarded conflict scholarships
summary:
Two Ukrainian wine professionals who were forced to leave their country following Russia’s invasion have been awarded the 2026 Artémis Domaines Golden Vines Victims of Conflict Scholarships. The programme will give both recipients fully funded six-month internships at estates within the Artémis Domaines portfolio.

The post Two Ukrainian wine professionals awarded conflict scholarships appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Two Ukrainian wine professionals who were forced to leave their country following Russia’s invasion have been awarded the 2026 Artémis Domaines Golden Vines Victims of Conflict Scholarships. The programme will give both recipients fully funded six-month internships at estates within the Artémis Domaines portfolio. Eleonora Kolieichik (left) and Anastasiia Igorevna Fedchenko (right) have been named as the recipients of the 2026 Artémis Domaines Golden Vines Victims of Conflict Scholarships. The initiative, created by Liquid Icons, the Gérard Basset Foundation and Artémis Domaines, supports wine professionals whose lives and careers have been disrupted by war and geopolitical conflict. Each recipient will receive a fully funded, bespoke six-month internship at one of the estates within the Artémis Domaines portfolio. These include Beaux-Frères, Bouchard Père & Fils, Champagne Jacquesson, Château Latour, Château-Grillet, Clos de Tart, Domaine d’Eugénie, Vignoble des Cabottes and Eisele Vineyard. Placements will be tailored to the scholars’ experience and long-term ambitions, with opportunities across viticulture, winemaking, production, hospitality, communications and marketing.

Rebuilding careers after war

Kolieichik, who is originally from eastern Ukraine, is now based in Paris, where she works as a sommelier and commercial assistant at Archives. Fedchenko, originally from Odesa, now lives in Tarragona and works as a wine data specialist at AVU Luxury Wine. Both were forced to leave Ukraine because of the war and rebuild their careers in another country. The pair were selected from 26 applicants, the highest number received since the programme began. Romané Basset, head of operations and co-founding trustee of the Gérard Basset Foundation, said: “This year’s scholarships attracted 26 applications from all over the world, the highest number since the programme was established, highlighting both the growing recognition of the initiative and the continuing need to support talented professionals whose careers have been interrupted by conflict. “The war in Ukraine may no longer dominate the daily news agenda, but for those whose lives and careers have been disrupted, its effects are obviously real and ongoing. These scholarships are about investing in talent, creating opportunity and ensuring that gifted wine professionals are not defined by the circumstances they have endured. “We are delighted to welcome Eleonora and Anastasiia to our community and look forward to working closely with each of them.”

Selected in two rounds

The recipients were chosen through a two-stage judging process led by Ian Harris MBE DipWSET, chair of the board of trustees of the Gérard Basset Foundation, and Jérémy Cukierman MW. Through their internships, Kolieichik and Fedchenko will gain practical experience at some of the world’s best-known wine estates, with each placement shaped around their professional backgrounds and future goals. The scholarships form part of the Gérard Basset Foundation’s wider aim to use education, mentorship and professional access to support people whose careers have been interrupted by conflict. The programme also continues the legacy of Gérard Basset, whose work centred on opening opportunities and nurturing talent across the international wine trade.

Ukrainian wine remains under pressure

As previously reported by the drinks business, Ukraine’s wine sector has continued to operate despite severe disruption since Russia’s full-scale invasion in February 2022. Producers have faced damage to vineyards and wineries, power shortages, labour disruption and reduced domestic consumption, particularly in the country’s southern wine regions. Svitlana Tsybak, CEO of Beykush Winery and president of the Ukrainian Association of Craft Winemakers, previously told db that the sector was operating under “constant uncertainty”. However, she also said the war had made Ukrainian wine more visible internationally, with growing interest from importers, sommeliers and consumers in the country’s indigenous varieties and emerging regional identity.

title: Have you tried the world’s first high fibre beer?
summary:
Scottish alcohol-free beer brand Brulo has launched its All Good IPA, a beer that delivers 5g of dietary fibre per can. db finds out more.

The post Have you tried the world’s first high fibre beer? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Scottish alcohol-free beer brand Brulo has launched its All Good IPA, a beer that delivers 5g of dietary fibre per can. db finds out more. The new 0.5% ABV IPA launches amid research revealing that 96% of UK adults fail to meet the recommended daily fibre intake. Brulo tackles this issue head-on by brewing the world's first beer to meet the official threshold for a high-in-fibre claim. The British government recommends 30g a day for adults, but, according to a Marks & Spencer report published earlier this year, the average intake is only around 16.2g, and 96% of the population falls short of the daily fibre recommendations.

'More fibre than a bowl of porridge'

All Good IPA delivers 5g of fibre per 330ml can—arguably more fibre than a bowl of porridge, two slices of wholemeal toast, or a medium banana—and roughly 17% of an adult's recommended daily intake in a single beer. The brewery explained that the beer started as a question. Although the alcohol-free category was racing toward sweeter, fruit-led styles, Brulo asked its customer base what they actually wanted next and the answer surprised the team. Talking to db about the consumer research, Brulo founder and CEO James Brown said: “I genuinely thought they'd ask for something fruity. Given where the category was heading, that's what I would have bet on. Instead, by a wide margin, they asked for something functional. A beer that gave back. I'd assumed they wanted indulgence. They wanted purpose. And they were specific about it: a proper beer, no fake flavouring, no sweet fruit bolted on to a base recipe as an afterthought. They wanted the real thing, with real benefits. Once we sat with it, it made complete sense. The people drinking alcohol free beer are already making a choice about their health. They were asking us to take that further.”

'Improved attention and focus'

According to Brulo, the fibre comes from a clinically researched soluble fibre with a growing body of evidence behind it. A peer-reviewed study published in The Journal of Nutrition in March 2026 by researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign already found that daily consumption of soluble fibre significantly improved response times in attention and focus tests for middle and older-aged adults. This seemed like a good place to start and the brewery pondered whether a fibre-filled non-alcoholic beer might actually help with focus. Earlier studies have shown the same fibre increases beneficial gut bacteria including bifidobacteria and supports the production of short-chain fatty acids, which the science community considers central to gut, immune and metabolic health. Added to this, a separate Purdue University study found soluble fibre could even potentially increase calcium absorption through changes in gut microbiota. Assessing the opportunity, Brown added: “Beer has always been the thing your body forgives you for. We thought it was time beer started giving something back. All Good IPA is a properly hoppy West Coast IPA that delivers more fibre than your breakfast. It tastes like the beer you want to drink and works like the food your body actually needs. Pints and porridge had a child, basically.” He explained to db that the launch caps 12 months of recipe development and pointed out that “getting 5g of soluble fibre into a hop-forward IPA without losing aroma or freshness was harder than it sounds”. Additionally, it became evident that the challenge was not body, which the fibre actually helps with, but ensuring the fibre survived the brewing and packaging process intact, did not break down over shelf life, and did not flatten the hop expression.

'Pours like an IPA, tastes like an IPA, and works like a fibre supplement'

Because of these tasks, Brulo ran extensive forced-ageing trials to confirm the fibre remained totally stable in the can over the long term. As such, the result is a beer that pours like an IPA, tastes like an IPA, and works like a fibre supplement. Brown added: “The functional alcohol free space is the fastest-growing area of the category, but a lot of the marketing in this space makes claims that aren't backed up. We almost called this beer All Good Prebiotic IPA before realising the word 'prebiotic' isn't an authorised health claim in the UK or EU. A surprising number of brands haven't been checked. We took a different approach: pick an ingredient with real clinical evidence, dose it at a level that actually delivers, and only make claims we can stand behind. The beer does the work, the science does the talking.” All Good IPA is available now via the Brulo beer webshop and retails at £2.62 for a 330ml can. The beer is vegan and brewed to just 0.5% ABV.

title: Aldi launches rosé colour swatch as shoppers reject the ‘wrong’ shade
summary:
Aldi has launched a limited-edition rosé colour guide after research found that nearly one in three UK adults had rejected a glass because it was not their preferred shade. The supermarket estimates that colour preferences account for more than £103 million worth of rosé being turned down each year.

The post Aldi launches rosé colour swatch as shoppers reject the ‘wrong’ shade appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Aldi has launched a limited-edition rosé colour guide after research found that nearly one in three UK adults had rejected a glass because it was not their preferred shade. The supermarket estimates that colour preferences account for more than £103 million worth of rosé being turned down each year. Aldi has created a pocket-sized Rosé Swatch keyring to help shoppers identify their preferred shade of pink wine before buying a bottle. The promotional launch follows research commissioned by the supermarket, which found that 31% of UK adults had refused a glass of rosé because of its colour. More than half of respondents, 56%, said colour was an important consideration when choosing a bottle, with 23% admitting that they always selected rosé according to its shade. Aldi estimated that the rejected glasses were collectively worth more than £103m each year.

Pale rosé remains the favourite

The research found that a light Provence-style blush was the most popular rosé colour, preferred by 40.9% of respondents. Pale salmon rosé followed with 20.6%, ahead of pale Italian pink at 6.4% and deeper-hued rosé at 6.3%. Some 13% of respondents said they had rejected a glass because it was too pale, with 18% doing so because it was too dark. A further 18% said they had wanted to refuse a glass due to its colour but accepted it out of politeness. Choosing for somebody else appeared to bring its own difficulties. Aldi found that 29% did not trust another person to select the right shade, with 32% describing buying rosé for somebody else as stressful. Partners were regarded as the safest people to entrust with the purchase, although only 31% believed theirs would make the correct choice.

Sales rise during hot weather

The campaign comes as rosé continues to outperform parts of the wider UK wine market. Two thirds of those surveyed said summer was the season in which they were most likely to drink rosé. The average drinker expected to consume 20 glasses over the summer, paying an average of £6.40 per glass or £15.60 per bottle. The most popular time for the first glass was 8pm. Aldi said sales of Chassaux et Fils Sainte Victoire Rosé had doubled year-on-year, with Costellore Primitivo Rosé up 64%. Julie Ashfield, chief commercial officer at Aldi UK, said: “As soon as the weather warms up, we see shoppers reaching for rosé, and this recent spell of sunshine has helped drive another fantastic summer for the range, with popular rosé bottles’ sales increasing year-on-year. “Our research shows people have surprisingly strong opinions about the colour of their rosé, with many admitting they’ve rejected a glass simply because it wasn’t the shade they were hoping for. The Rosé Swatch is a fun way to take the guesswork out of choosing rosé while encouraging shoppers to discover that every shade has something different to offer.”

Colour does not determine quality

Aldi wine expert Sam Caporn MW cautioned drinkers against treating pale rosé as inherently superior. “Many of us think a pale rosé is automatically ‘better’, but that’s just not true. The colour is about style, not quality,” she said. “Paler hues are usually lighter and more delicate, while darker rosés, such as those made from Primitivo or Syrah, can have deeper fruit flavours and often pair beautifully with food, so don’t be afraid to explore the full colour spectrum.” Caporn also argued that adding ice should not be regarded as a serious rosé offence. “Despite half, 51%, of Brits saying they wouldn’t dream of it, the cold temperature softens acidity for a smoother, incredibly refreshing finish,” she said. She recommended drinking rosé young, particularly fresher styles from Provence, and encouraged consumers to experiment with garnishes including berries, strawberry and jalapeño.

Confusion over rosé production

Despite rosé’s popularity, Aldi’s research suggested considerable uncertainty over how it is made. More than half of respondents, 56%, incorrectly believed that rosé was produced by mixing red and white grapes, with 16% thinking it came from a distinct variety of “rosé grapes”. Drinkers identified 6.9°C as the ideal serving temperature and generally believed bottles should be chilled for between one and three hours. As previously reported by the drinks business, rosé has provided one of the few areas of growth within the struggling UK still wine market. UK rosé volumes rose 3% over the previous year, with value sales increasing 5% to £882m. Consumers bought the equivalent of 129 million bottles during 2025, compared with 125m in 2024. The growth has been led primarily by affordable French brands priced below £10, including La Vieille Ferme, La P’tite Pierre, Le Bijou de Sophie Valrose and Studio by Miraval. Aldi’s limited-edition Rosé Swatch will be distributed through a giveaway, with entries accepted by email until 11 August 2026.

title: What’s happening to Sheffield’s historic Cannon Brewery?
summary:
The Cannon Brewery, based in Neepsend in Sheffield, has had planning approved to be turned into a new neighbourhood. db looks at the details.

The post What’s happening to Sheffield’s historic Cannon Brewery? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The Cannon Brewery, based in Neepsend in Sheffield, has had planning approved to be turned into a new neighbourhood. db looks at the details. The 4.2-acre brownfield site, which is also home to where Stones Bitter was brewed for many years, will be turned into a £150–£200 million mixed-use development by the developers Capital&Centric.

An historic landmark

The historic industrial landmark has been sitting completely derelict for more than 25 years and has now secured official planning permission to transform the site and its buildings into an urban community The production site, which began as the Neepsend Brewery in 1838, originally operated by independent brewers Shepherd, Green & Hatfield. Then, in 1868, brewer William Stones bought the lease and renamed it the Cannon Brewery. In 1948, the site began brewing Stones Bitter which, by the nineties had grown in popularity and reached national acclaim in 1992 when it was named the UK’s bestselling bitter.

Restoration of symbolic brew house and water tower

Sadly, following corporate acquisitions by Bass and later Interbrew, brewing on the site ceased in 1999, leaving the brewery buildings abandoned and the derelict site fell into disrepair. The grain store was demolished after being deemed unsafe and yet the brewhouse and water tower were ripe for restoration. Now, with plans approved for 500 new homes, businesses, cafés, bars and public green spaces, the brewery site is being readied for building work to take place. According to the developers, the first stage of the project will see 240 new apartments built before the full 500 are completed. Alongside the housing, the scheme will also play host to small businesses, artists and makers, with around 15,000 sq ft of commercial space planned for the development project.

Keeping the character

Capital&Centric has said that the aim is to create a place where people can not only live, but also work and socialise, while still keeping the character of the historic brewery site. Residents living on site, buying into any of the new properties, will have access to shared facilities including a gym, co-working spaces, a residents’ lounge and a private cinema. Plus, the site also recently welcomed Sheffield’s first indoor padel club Club de Padel. Capital&Centric joint managing director John Moffat said: “This place has been part of Sheffield’s story for generations and we are excited and proud to give it a new lease of life. We are creating a new neighbourhood in one of Sheffield’s fastest-growing districts whilst celebrating heritage and history with hundreds of new homes, green public spaces, vibrant workspace and somewhere people can live, work and socialise.”

title: Gusbourne launches exclusive British Airways sparkling wine
summary:
Gusbourne has created a bespoke English sparkling wine for British Airways Club World, as the Kent producer continues to expand its international footprint across more than 40 export markets.

The post Gusbourne launches exclusive British Airways sparkling wine appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Gusbourne has created a bespoke English sparkling wine for British Airways Club World, as the Kent producer continues to expand its international footprint across more than 40 export markets.

Gusbourne has launched a new English sparkling wine created exclusively for British Airways Club World, marking another step in the Kent producer's international expansion.

From July 2026, Gusbourne Airbourne Reserve 2021 will be served in British Airways Club World cabins. The estate said the listing comes as it now exports to more than 40 markets worldwide and continues to build its presence as an ambassador for English sparkling wine.

The producer described Airbourne Reserve as a "genuinely limited expression" of the estate, created specifically for British Airways.

Created for service at altitude

Gusbourne wines have been listed onboard British Airways since 2025. Passengers travelling in First currently receive Fifty One Degrees North 2016, while Club World has served Sparkling Rosé 2019.

Airbourne Reserve 2021 is a Pinot Noir-dominant sparkling wine produced specifically for the airline. According to Gusbourne, the wine is richer, rounder and more textural than many English sparkling wines. It was selected for the British Airways listing by Tim Jackson MW, who chose it for its performance at altitude.

The bottle design also references British aviation heritage. Gusbourne said the label features Airforce Blue, a herringbone deboss inspired by the twill of original Royal Air Force uniforms and the RAF roundel.

Export growth

The British Airways listing forms part of Gusbourne's wider international strategy. The estate said it now exports to more than 40 countries and is continuing to expand through travel retail, helping introduce English sparkling wine to consumers in overseas markets.

Leading the producer's international expansion is Duncan Brown, Head of Exports and Travel Retail. Brown also sits on the Wine GB Export Council, where he helps shape export strategy for the wider English wine sector.

"For us, every bottle that leaves these shores is an opportunity to represent not just Gusbourne, but English sparkling wine as a category. When Airbourne is poured in Club World on a British Airways flight, passengers are experiencing what this country is capable of producing. That is a responsibility we take seriously - and an opportunity we intend to build on," said Brown.

Norway remains key export market

Gusbourne also highlighted Norway as its largest export success story.

The producer said it had no presence in the country nine years ago. Today, Norway accounts for around 40% of Gusbourne's export volume, while more than half of all English sparkling wine exported internationally goes to Norway.

According to the estate, the market has evolved from having little awareness of English sparkling wine to becoming one where the category is now an established choice.

The producer also pointed to a series of recent accolades, including becoming the first English producer to win the Chairman's Trophy at the Champagne & Sparkling Wine World Championships, receiving six CSWWC Gold Medals and three CSWWC Best in Class trophies, winning Walpole Luxury Brand of the Year, seeing Fifty One Degrees North 2016 named No.2 in Wine Enthusiast's Top 100 Best Wines of 2025 and awarded 98 points by the publication, and receiving the King's Award for Enterprise in recognition of sustained export growth.

title: How artificial intelligence is safeguarding a millennia-old practice
summary:
Futuristic machine learning and Iberian cork forests may seem poles apart, but M.A.Silva is using AI to offer ever more precision and reliability to winemakers.

The post How artificial intelligence is safeguarding a millennia-old practice appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Futuristic machine learning and Iberian cork forests may seem poles apart, but M.A.Silva is using AI to offer ever more precision and reliability to winemakers. Cork and wine have been intertwined for a long time. There is evidence of it sealing amphorae in the ancient world and, while it fell out of fashion until the early modern period, it has been the most popular closure for more than 300 years. AI is much newer on the scene. Its story can be measured only in decades. In the form we most interact with, it has been years. Yet the two fields are now working in tandem. Cork producer M.A.Silva is using high-tech AI models to assist in the production of its Nobeltech corks. The top-of-the line closures employ Bionic Eye, an integrated AI system, as one of three core technologies assuring their quality. The fundamentals of the company, at a glance, look the same. M.A.Silva still prides itself on quality rather than scale, and still operates a vertical production to ensure sustainability and control. Yet AI is pushing the boundaries in terms of completeness, consistency and predictability. The corks may look no different, but it is facilitating a quiet revolution that provides winemakers with the ultimate guarantee for their wines.

Inspection from every angle

Checking corks for quality and consistency has always been key to the cork industry’s success. As a natural product, there will always be variability, and so both employees and automated optical inspection systems have been used to find those corks that do not meet M.A.Silva’s rigorous requirements. AI is taking on the same job. Its scale and its accuracy, however, represent a step change. M.A.Silva’s AI-powered inspection system runs multiple independent inspections of a cork, assessing it from every angle and across a range of parameters. Ahead of the final classification of a Nobeltech cork, 12 inspections are made. Taken together, they offer a judgment on TCA (the compound behind cork taint), mechanical performance and sealing; in short, M.A.Silva can assure winemakers that any cork passing classification will operate effectively and maintain the wine’s character. Unlike human checkers, such a system is unaffected by fatigue and can adapt seamlessly to different conditions. While M.A.Silva has found that human consistency in such checks hovers around 75%, the AI system is close to 100% consistent. Where it has the edge on previous machines is in its capacity for deep learning. It is not bound by an existing algorithm – instead, as it encounters more of the product, it finds nuances in assessment. The precision with which the AI system can detect faults is remarkable. It can spot structural cracks, clay contamination, lenticel exposure and even insect holes (both open and closed). As new patterns emerge, the model adapts, and that information is never forgotten. For a producer seeking to safeguard their production, therefore, M.A.Silva’s AI-powered system offers more assurance than ever.

Improving the product

Those advances – essentially, doing the work of checking corks more effectively than ever before – is a selling point in itself. But it is not the end of the advance. M.A.Silva is discovering ways in which AI is expanding the possibilities in cork production. While reliability is paramount, uniformity in a cork batch is also crucial. Over a short period ageing in the bottle – as long as the seal holds – the oxygen transmission rate (OTR) will not be hugely significant. Yet over an extended period, especially when it is a fine wine, the OTR is bound up with the wine’s performance. The slow ingress of oxygen is a key determiner for tertiary development, one of the key determiners of quality in the world’s best wines. Thus, a consistent cork is desirable. If the OTR is the same across a batch, the wines should age in a uniform manner. M.A.Silva’s research indicates that AI-selected corks are highly consistent in their OTR after one year, and that the wines’ taste profile is likewise consistent. In practice, the machine learning can extrapolate performance based on past data, predicting performance over longer periods. That moves cork assessment from aesthetic judgment to a results-oriented process. If long-term consistency in OTR is the goal, AI is meaning that M.A.Silva can select for that. Moreover, as the ultimate guarantee, the AI system delivers traceability. Even though the system works at an immense scale, its work can be tracked. It is possible for M.A.Silva to produce up to 40,000 corks per hour (more than 11 every second), and yet every single inspection is recorded independently. Each stopper receives multiple documented quality decisions, including imaging and classification, ensuring that the entire process is tracked. That traceability of Nobeltech corks proves the difference, and offers an assurance that M.A.Silva has done its work. Moreover, it proves the value when the improvements may not be visible. There is no sci-fi adornment, no microchip in the closure. The AI-powered system is a great leap forward that actually means the traditional means of production – a natural, sustainable product – remain the same. The difference is an ever stronger promise that M.A.Silva will deliver quality. That is the remarkable union of AI and cork – the industry of the moment protecting a historic trade.

title: Harvest begins in Roussillon
summary:
Domaine Lafage has begun its earliest-ever harvest following an exceptionally early growing season in Roussillon.

The post Harvest begins in Roussillon appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Domaine Lafage has begun its earliest-ever harvest following an exceptionally early growing season in Roussillon.

Among the first estates in the region to start harvesting each year, Domaine Lafage started picking today (Thursday 23 July).

The unusually early start to the 2026 harvest has been driven by a combination of factors. Budburst occurred around 15 days earlier than normal, accelerating the growing season, while generous rainfall early in the year replenished water reserves. This was followed by an exceptional weather pattern, with consistently high temperatures throughout spring and summer and extreme heat recorded from May onwards.

The first grapes to be picked are Muscat Petits Grains from vineyards along the Mediterranean coast. Harvested at optimal ripeness, they will be used to produce the Lafage family's lightly sparkling white wine, Lafabuleuse, which is once again expected to have a moderate alcohol level of around 9% ABV.

A pioneer of regenerative viticulture, Domaine Lafage has had its entire vineyard estate certified by A Greener World (AGW) since the beginning of 2026. Over several years, the Lafage family has developed a range of practices aimed at improving vineyard resilience to climate change while enhancing biodiversity across its diverse terroirs. These include innovative water management strategies, soil regeneration through compost and biochar applications, and agroforestry initiatives, all designed to create healthier, more resilient vineyard ecosystems.

Following several vintages affected by severe drought, the 2026 season has brought renewed optimism to Roussillon. Winter and spring rainfall has helped restore balance to the vineyards, creating favourable conditions for a healthy, high-quality harvest with strong potential.

title: Champagne to limit production to 250m bottles for 2026
summary:
The region of Champagne is to cap the amount of wine that can be made from this year’s harvest at 8,800kg/ha ­– the equivalent of around 250 million bottles.

The post Champagne to limit production to 250m bottles for 2026 appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The region of Champagne is to cap the amount of wine that can be made from this year’s harvest at 8,800kg/ha ­– the equivalent of around 250 million bottles.

Set by the Comité Champagne (CIVC), the figure represents the “rendement commercialisable” or “marketable yield”, and this year’s limit is the lowest in the modern era of the appellation, with the exception of 2020’s Covid-induced anomaly – when the region capped production at 8,000kg/ha (representing fewer than 230m bottles). The limit for this year’s harvest is lower than in 2025 (9,000kg/ha), as well as 2024, when it was capped at 10,000kg/ha, which, in turn, was down a little more than 12% on the 2023 vintage, when it was set at 11,400kg/ha (and almost 17% lower than in 2022, when it was 12,000kg/ha).

A 'collective' decision

Justifying the decision to set this figure, which comes after cuts in the three preceding harvests, the heads of the Comité Champagne stressed that the limit was set by the grape growers and winemakers to prevent the region from producing more wine than it can sell. In a statement released by the Comité Champagne yesterday, it stated, “This collective decision aims to gradually rebalance stock, while preserving the economic viability of the vineyards and maintaining quality standards.” Commenting on demand for the sparkling wine, it recorded a 1.2% growth in shipments for the first half of this year, with a total of 107.1 million bottles from January to June, with exports driving the increase. Should that trend continue, the region would ship 269m bottles by the year-end, up on last year's total of 266m, which itself was a 2% decrease compared with 271m bottles in 2024. “Champagne has a unique collective model that allows it to adapt its decisions to market realities without losing sight of what matters most: preserving the appellation’s value over the long term,” said David Chatillon, president of the Union des Maisons de Champagne and co-president of the Comité Champagne Continuing he said, “In an economic climate that remains uncertain, and as the industry continues to gradually rebalance its stock, this yield reflects a responsible approach.”

A complex harvest

Also commenting on the figure, Maxime Toubart, president of the Syndicat Général des Vignerons de la Champagne and co-president of the Comité Champagne, said, “The yield set this year reflects a measured decision, mindful of both the reality of the vineyards and the future of the industry.” In terms of harvest conditions, the growing season was described as “complex”, marked by episodes of frost, extreme heat in June and the recent onset of drought, with significant variation seen across Champagne's vineyards. It is expected that the harvest will take place between 20 and 25 August on average – which is 10 to 15 days earlier than usual – with the official start of the harvest is scheduled for 15 August. As previously reported by db, following an unusually early and heavily frost-damaged start to the season, followed by recent heatwave conditions, Champagne could be on course for the earliest start date for harvesting in its long history.

title: Leopard attacks Rajasthan liquor store cashier
summary:
A leopard prowled into a liquor store in Rajasthan, northwestern India, and proceeded to pounce on a panicked shopkeeper before roaming around the store, knocking over bottles of booze, CCTV footage shows.

The post Leopard attacks Rajasthan liquor store cashier appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
A leopard prowled into a liquor store in Rajasthan, northwestern India, and proceeded to pounce on a panicked shopkeeper before roaming around the store, knocking over bottles of booze, CCTV footage shows. Colleagues in the shop in Todaraisingh, Rajasthan can be heard shrieking in the dramatic video footage, before the wild cat suddenly lunges at 25-year-old worker, Sanjay Gurjar.  The cashier is seen wrestling with the fierce feline, before he manages to push it off, leap over the table, and flee. The leopard then moves through the store, knocking over bottles of spirits. Just moments before entering the shop the animal reportedly attacked bystander Fateh Lal Koli, 40, while he was sitting outside the alcohol store.

Aftermath of the attack

Both victims of the attack are now receiving treatment for their injuries in hospital. The feline, thought to be a five-year-old male, was tranquilised by a specialist animal unit, who are set to perform medical checks before releasing him back into the wild. While many leopards live in Todaraising’s surrounding forests, they usually avoid human contact unless injured or threatened.

title: Diageo’s India unit reports 52% leap in quarterly profit
summary:
United Spirits, Diageo's Indian subsidiary, has reported a sharp rise in first-quarter profit, fuelled by robust demand for its premium spirits portfolio.

The post Diageo’s India unit reports 52% leap in quarterly profit appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
United Spirits, Diageo's Indian subsidiary, has reported a sharp rise in first-quarter profit, fuelled by robust demand for its premium spirits portfolio. The spirits maker and distributor of brands including Johnnie Walker, Antiquity, Black & White whisky and Tanqueray gin said standalone net profit rose 51.6% to 3.91bn rupees (£30.27m) in the three months to 30 June, up from 2.58bn rupees (£19.98m) rupees a year earlier. Revenue for the quarter increased 5% year-on-year to 61.13bn rupees, while operating expenses rose 4.6%. Growth was led by the company's premium portfolio, with net sales in the segment rising 10.1% from a year earlier. Premium brands accounted for 91.7% of United Spirits' net sales during the quarter. Commenting on the company's performance, Praveen Someshwar, CEO and managing director, United Spirits, said, "We have commenced fiscal 2027 on a strong note with double-digit growth in the Prestige & Above segment. "Our consumer centric interventions give us confidence to increase growth further as the year progresses. We continue to future-proof our portfolio while creating enduring value for all our stakeholders."

Karnataka reforms

Analysts at Jefferies had expected the company to benefit from improved performance of its McDowell's brand following a relaunch, as well as recent tax changes in the southern Indian state of Karnataka. Karnataka, one of India's largest alcohol markets, announced in March that it would abolish government price controls and introduce a strength-based excise taxation system from April. The changes, which took effect on 11 May, allow alcohol producers greater flexibility to set prices while reducing tax rates on premium spirits. The reforms are expected to support demand for higher-end liquor brands in the state.

title: Could craft beer be seeing the start of a second wave?
summary:
In the US, craft brewing trends have held steady, declines in operating breweries have slightly increased and consumer engagement has strengthened. Jessica Mason asks: are we seeing craft beer’s resurgence?

The post Could craft beer be seeing the start of a second wave? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
In the US, craft brewing trends have held steady, declines in operating breweries have slightly increased and consumer engagement has strengthened. Jessica Mason asks: are we seeing craft beer’s resurgence? The Brewers Association (BA) released results from its midyear survey, presenting an updated look at the independent beer sector in the US. Despite the findings still pointing to continued pressure, there are however several indicators to suggest the contraction may be moderating. Among the analysis, the BA estimates craft volume declined 4% in the first six months of 2026, compared to the same period in 2025. But the overall decline of craft volume is not the only story in the data, because, looking more closely, it was identified that among survey respondents, 54% reported growth, 43% reported declines, and 3% remained flat.

Seeing a shift

In fact, every craft brewery type had a greater share reporting growth than decline, with taprooms (57% growing) and regional breweries (56% growing) leading the charge. Among breweries producing more than 10,000 barrels, 59% reported growth and just 40% reported declines. While the BA admitted that there’s likely positive-leaning sample bias since breweries are more likely to report when things are going well, this is still an overall bump from 49% reporting growth in the same survey last year. Speaking to the drinks business, Brewers Association staff economist Matt Gacioch said: "It's too early to say craft beer has definitively turned a corner, but trends tend to steady out before they shift direction. The data suggests that this may be where the industry finds itself today." Craft consumers offered another encouraging signal. In the 2026 BA and Harris Poll Consumer Survey of more than 2,000 adults, monthly craft consumption among craft drinkers reached 85%, up 10 percentage points year-over-year and up to its highest level since 2019–2020. Craft drinkers also visited breweries 5.5 times on average over the past year, up from 5.1 in 2025.

'Cautious optimism'

Within the report, the BA remained cautious when approaching the possibility of a turnaround with too much hope, but the initial signs are looking positive. Instead of giving a completely optimistic view, Gacioch tentatively said that “in spite of these bright spots, it is too soon to say whether craft has reached the bottom of the valley for trends in production volume or number of breweries”. But the data should give brewers a lift, especially since there are many out there who have been battered by the onslaught of headwinds the sector has faced. As Gacioch explained: “Still, after several years of figures moving in the wrong direction, that moderation may catalyse some cautious optimism for an industry of perseverant brewers.” Gacioch admitted that “the world in which craft brewers operate hasn’t gotten any easier or more predictable in 2026, but perhaps the breweries that have weathered the storm so far are the ones that have best positioned themselves for storm weathering into the future”. Granted, the number of operating breweries also continued to decline. For instance, there were 9,344 breweries operating in June 2026, down 1.8% from 9,515 a year earlier in June 2025. This represents a slight uptick in the rate of decline, from -1% in the first half of 2025. The number of regional breweries and microbreweries each declined 3%, followed by taprooms at 2% and brewpubs at 1%. However, because taprooms and brewpubs are far more numerous, the BA pointed out that those smaller percentage changes still represent meaningful absolute numbers of closures.

Breweries with taprooms were best-performing

For the first half of 2026, taprooms were the best-performing brewery type by volume change, outpacing other models. Additionally, when assessing by channel, distributed draught beat distributed packaged products and onsite sales. The BA noted that this suggests craft brewers are finding opportunities to meet consumer needs in draught amid wholesaler consolidation and portfolio rationalisation. Even though retail scan data showed steeper declines than the BA estimate, for instance, off-trade data from NielsenIQ (NIQ) has shown BA-defined craft down 5.2% in the first half of 2026. By comparison, NIQ-defined craft was down 5.6% for the same period, and beer plus non-alcohol beer was down 4%. Looking at the bigger picture, there is still a long journey ahead. After all, beer continues to experience declines alongside other beverage alcohol categories. This can be seen from Wine & Spirits Wholesalers of America (WSWA) SipSource data which showed spirits volume down 5.3% and wine down 9.2% for the 12 months ending May 2026. As the report flagged, even though that period does not align perfectly with the first-half craft beer figures, it does offer the most recent comparison available.

title: All the medallists from The Global Pinot Gris Masters 2026
summary:
Pinot Gris is often associated with undemanding wines, but in the right hands it is capable of producing something magical, says Patricia Stefanowicz MW.

The post All the medallists from The Global Pinot Gris Masters 2026 appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Pinot Gris is often associated with undemanding wines, but in the right hands it is capable of producing something magical, says Patricia Stefanowicz MW. “NOTHING IS so easy as to deceive oneself; for what we wish we readily believe,” the Athenian statesman and orator Demosthenes warns us in his Third Olynthiac. Many wine people tend to think of almost all Pinot Grigio – and much Pinot Gris – as vaguely fruity, neutral white wines to be chosen by consumers who would like a glass of inexpensive, easy-drinking white wine with not much varietal flavour. Thus the judges, on a cool and drizzly London morning, approached this year’s Global Pinot Gris Masters with notions of impending dullness rather than with anticipated excitement. A lesson to be learnt for occasionally somnolent judges? Never underestimate what might come your way. In fact, it was a truly successful judging day. The vast majority of wines were really delightful – fresh, pure and quaffable. What this Global Masters showed is that Pinot Grigio/Pinot Gris can provide, as one judge noted, “uncomplicated drinkability at sensible prices”, if not the great complexity of fine-quality Chardonnay or Riesling. An intriguing aspect of this year’s judging was the subtle variation in fruit flavours the wines delivered. Perhaps the most surprising thing was how well the really inexpensive wines (under £10) performed. There were Silver medals aplenty, and a Gold for Tenuta La Presa’s Pinot Grigio Rosé. At £10–£15, most of the white wines came from Italy, with the Veneto DOC in preponderance, although there were some from Trentino-Alto Adige and even Sicily, and a few from Australia and New Zealand. The wines have bright orchard fruit and melon, supported by zippy acidity, which meant that most of the wines achieved Silvers. Gold medals went to Cavit from Alto Adige, Corvezzo from the Veneto, and Marisco from Marlborough.

REAL EXCITEMENT

The judges found real excitement at £15–£20 – the wines offered exceptional value for money in today’s marketplace. Unoaked whites from Torresella in the Veneto and De Bortoli in Victoria, Australia, were awarded Golds. In the oaked white group, Austria took a Gold for Nestor Wein’s Burgenland Pinot Gris and our Grand Master for the incomparable Krispel Grauburgunder from Vulkanland-Steiermark. Within the higher price range of £20–£30, there was nothing but shimmering awards. Vibrant and balanced, Ca’ Bolani’s unoaked wine from Friuli in Italy and Krispel’s oaked version from Vulkanland-Steiermark in Austria both won Golds, and an unoaked wine from Nals Margreid in Alto Adige was awarded a Master. Our most expensive price bracket in this year’s competition was £30–£50, where all the wines achieved at least a Gold medal. Oaked expressions Vie di Romans Dessimis from Friuli and Mezzacorona Musivum from Trentino were joined by Riversdale Estate’s unoaked Pinot Gris from Tasmania. Krispel from Austria again came up trumps, with its oaked Grauburgunder being awarded a Master. Weingut Krispel deserves a special mention for its hat-trick for the three vintages of Grauburgunder, which took home a Gold, a Master and the Grand Master in this year’s event. We are fairly certain that these bottles should be on every discerning wine drinker’s list. As the judges discovered, there is more to Pinot Grigio/Pinot Gris than simple quaffing wines destined for the pub or a backyard barbecue. Many of the wines have succulent richness, balanced by just enough acidity. True excitement, even at knock-down prices, is not only achievable – it is here and now.
