title: Wetherspoon sales rise but World Cup boost fails to offset higher costs
summary:
JD Wetherspoon has warned that annual profits will fall below market expectations despite a 4% increase in like-for-like sales during the final quarter. Analysts said the World Cup probably brought more customers into its pubs, but rising food, labour and energy costs continued to outpace revenue growth.

The post Wetherspoon sales rise but World Cup boost fails to offset higher costs appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
JD Wetherspoon has warned that annual profits will fall below market expectations despite a 4% increase in like-for-like sales during the final quarter. Analysts said the World Cup probably brought more customers into its pubs, but rising food, labour and energy costs continued to outpace revenue growth. JD Wetherspoon has issued a fresh profit warning after slightly weaker than expected fourth-quarter sales combined with higher operating costs. Like-for-like sales increased by 4% in the 12 weeks to 19 July 2026, with year-to-date growth reaching 4.2%. However, chairman Tim Martin said: “Profits for the year are likely to be below market expectations, with marginally lower sales than anticipated in the final quarter, combined with higher costs in the areas of food, labour, repairs, energy and business rates.” The company will publish its preliminary results on 2 October.

Football fans provide limited relief

Duncan Ferris, analyst at investment platform Freetrade, said Wetherspoon was likely to have benefited from customers watching England during the World Cup, although the additional sales were not enough to protect profitability. “Things were short and decidedly not sweet from JD Wetherspoon this morning, with the pub chain once again warning on profits. “Sales are higher, likely aided by punters packing out pubs to watch the Three Lions, but the real competition is between sales and costs. Like-for-like growth of 4% might be an improvement on the previous quarter, but it still represents a deceleration from the same period last year. “Rival chains Marston’s and Fuller’s have already hailed the impact of the tournament on sales. Wetherspoon probably enjoyed its own World Cup boost, but thirsty football fans clearly were not enough to stop final-quarter sales from disappointing.” As previously reported by the drinks business, football delivered an uneven benefit for drinks companies and hospitality operators during the tournament. Morgan Stanley reduced its estimate of the World Cup’s contribution to annual global beer volumes from 24 basis points to 17 basis points after several major markets, including Brazil and Germany, exited earlier than expected. Carlsberg emerged as one of the stronger beneficiaries due to the progress of teams in markets where it has significant exposure.

Costs squeeze slender margins

Ferris said Wetherspoon’s value-led model left the business particularly vulnerable to inflation across several parts of its cost base. “This morning’s rather terse update makes things plain: rising costs are outpacing revenue growth. “As a chain that prioritises affordability, Wetherspoon operates on relatively slender margins. These are being squeezed from almost every direction, with Tim Martin listing food, labour, repairs, energy, and business rates as the stressors swallowing its profits.” The chain currently operates 793 managed pubs after opening eight and selling nine during the financial year. It has also opened 15 franchised pubs, taking its franchised estate to 23.

Could live sport become a bigger part of the offer?

Unlike many competing pub groups, Wetherspoon has historically avoided paying for broad subscriptions to services such as Sky Sports and TNT Sports across its estate. The World Cup, which was available through free-to-air television, gave the group an opportunity to attract football audiences without incurring the subscription costs associated with year-round domestic and European competitions. The strength of trade during major tournaments may raise the question of whether selected Wetherspoon venues could benefit from showing more live sport outside international competitions. Such a shift would come with considerable costs and could alter the atmosphere of pubs that currently position themselves around affordable food and drink rather than televised sport. Ferris said the immediate issue remained converting customer demand into stronger returns. “Wetherspoon’s busy pubs mean its value proposition is resonating with customers, but the ultimate goal is converting rising sales into rising profits.”

Debt guidance improves

Wetherspoon expects year-end net debt to stand at £720 million, in line with the end of the previous financial year and better than earlier guidance. The company has bought 6.4 million of its own shares for cancellation during the year at an average price of £6.52, spending almost £42m. It also spent £12.2m acquiring the freehold reversions of four pubs, bringing total expenditure on freehold reversions since 2011 to £489m. Ferris said: “The balance sheet offers a sliver of light. Year-end net debt of £720m is better than previous guidance even after Wetherspoon spent almost £42m buying back its own shares.”

title: India tariff cut opens door to wider Scotch whisky market
summary:
The UK–India Free Trade Agreement has reduced India’s tariff on Scotch whisky from 150% to 75%, giving producers greater access to the world’s largest whisky market. The duty is due to fall progressively to 40% over the next decade.

The post India tariff cut opens door to wider Scotch whisky market appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
The UK–India Free Trade Agreement has reduced India’s tariff on Scotch whisky from 150% to 75%, giving producers greater access to the world’s largest whisky market. The duty is due to fall progressively to 40% over the next decade. The UK–India Free Trade Agreement came into force on 15 July, immediately halving India’s import tariff on Scotch whisky and removing one of the industry’s most persistent barriers to growth. The duty has fallen from 150% to 75%, with a further phased reduction to 40% planned over the next ten years. Although the remaining tariff is still considerable, the agreement is expected to improve the competitiveness and availability of Scotch whisky in a market where imported products have traditionally faced prohibitive costs. John Kennedy, managing director at Decant Index, said: “The initial impact is likely to include improved margins for established exporters and greater scope for Indian distributors to introduce a wider selection of Scotch whisky. Smaller and independent producers that may previously have found the cost of entering India prohibitive could also gain access to the market. “Over time, this could result in greater availability of premium single malts, age-stated expressions, independent bottlings and limited-edition releases.”

Largest whisky market

India is already Scotch whisky’s largest export market by volume and the biggest whisky market in the world. More than 220 million equivalent 70cl bottles of Scotch were exported to India in 2025, although Scotch still accounts for only around 3% of the country’s total whisky market. Exports were worth £286 million last year, up 15% on 2024. Volumes also rose by 15%, making India Scotch whisky’s third-largest export destination by value. Kennedy said: “By reducing one of the industry’s most significant trade barriers, the agreement should improve competitiveness, margins and market access for Scotch whisky producers in a market that had already grown.” The Scotch Whisky Association has described the agreement as “a once in a generation deal and a landmark moment for Scotch whisky”. Mark Kent, chief executive of the association, previously referred to the “strategic importance of this landmark deal for the long-term growth” of the sector, arguing that it should support further investment and employment in both India and the UK.

Opportunities beyond large exporters

The tariff reduction is expected to benefit multinational groups already established in India, but it may also provide a route into the market for independent distillers and bottlers. Smaller producers have historically struggled to absorb the cost of a 150% import duty, particularly when competing against locally made whisky and global brands with established distribution networks. Lower tariffs could give Indian importers more freedom to expand their ranges and introduce products that had previously been too expensive to list. The agreement also covers bulk Scotch, a substantial proportion of which is shipped to India for local bottling or use within Indian whisky brands. Kennedy said: “The most immediate benefit will be felt by companies exporting bottled and bulk whisky. Stronger consumer demand can, however, feed back through the wider Scotch whisky supply chain.”

What it could mean for casks

Greater demand from India could eventually increase requirements for young malt and grain whisky, blending stock, age-stated single malt and liquid intended for independent or limited-edition releases. “A growing Indian market could create additional requirements for young malt and grain whisky used within blends, mature blending stock, age-stated single malt and casks suitable for independent, bespoke or limited-edition bottlings,” Kennedy said. “A significant proportion of the whisky exported to India is already shipped in bulk, some for bottling as Scotch whisky and much of it for use within Indian whisky products. Reduced tariffs may therefore benefit Scottish suppliers as well as Indian producers that require imported Scotch for their own brands.” He cautioned, however, that the agreement would not necessarily translate into an immediate rise in cask values. “This does not mean that cask values will automatically rise as a direct consequence of the agreement. Individual values will continue to depend on the distillery, age, cask type, alcohol strength, remaining liquid volume, provenance, contractual rights and wider market conditions. “The more meaningful long-term benefit is the potential expansion of the buyer base and the number of commercial uses for maturing Scotch whisky.”

Indian producers prepare for competition

As previously reported by the drinks business, the agreement has been widely welcomed by Scotch producers. However, Indian distillers have raised concerns about the potential effect of cheaper imports on the domestic industry. When the deal was announced, William Wemyss, managing director of Wemyss Family Spirits, said the tariff reduction would create a “fairer footing” for businesses that had previously been “locked out of meaningful access” to India. Jean-Etienne Gourgues, chairman and CEO of Chivas Brothers, described the agreement as a “game changer” for Scotch exports. Indian producers have taken a more cautious position. Paul John warned that a sudden influx of lower-priced Scotch could affect domestic sales, although the company later said it was confident in the quality of Indian whisky and hoped the agreement would improve access for Indian brands entering the UK. The wider trade package will reduce or remove tariffs on 90% of UK goods entering India and 99% of Indian goods entering the UK. The UK Government estimates that the agreement could eventually increase bilateral trade by £25.5 billion a year, adding £4.8bn to UK GDP and £5.1bn to India’s economy.

title: Business rates cut by new Prime Minister
summary:
Pubs, clubs and live music venues across England are set to receive a 20% cut to business rates bills from next April. The move has welcomed by the beer and hospitality sector, offer it "breathing space" and assist in reviving community-focused businesses in high streets across England. db reports.

The post Business rates cut by new Prime Minister appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Pubs, clubs and live music venues across England are set to receive a 20% cut to business rates bills from next April. The move has welcomed by the beer and hospitality sector, offer it "breathing space" and assist in reviving community-focused businesses in high streets across England. db reports. The action was taken by new Prime Minister Andy Burnham who began his tenure this week and has only been in the role for three days, but has long-stated his intentions to build an economy that works for everyone.

Saving the hospitality sector

The decision, which will save the typical pub an estimated £1,100 next year, is part of a wider action plan from Burnham to save the hospitality sector and support local hubs. The action also forms part of a series of measures to cut costs for working people and communities, including electricity bill VAT relief and £2 bus fare cap. In its statements regarding the cuts, the Government highlighted that, for too long, the pubs, social clubs, and live music venues that form the backbone of local high streets have been replaced by boarded up windows and for sale signs. The announcement forms part of a wider plan from the PM to drive good growth in every postcode by reducing the burden on local businesses, boosting investment on high streets and helping communities to thrive.

Benefitting 'nearly 32,000 pubs, clubs and live music venues'

In a statement, the Government indicated that the new business rate cuts will “benefit nearly 32,000 pubs, clubs and live music venues, saving the typical pub an estimated £1,100 in the next financial year, providing much-needed certainty for businesses looking to invest, grow and create jobs”. The changes will be fully funded, including through reviewing reliefs for businesses that do not make a positive contribution to local communities, such as vape shops. Also, the Government has revealed that it will also crack down on businesses that sell through online marketplaces but do not comply with their tax obligations, putting them at an unfair advantage over businesses that play by the rules. According to the statements from Westminster, the Government is consulting on measures to make online marketplaces more responsible for preventing non-compliant sellers from avoiding their tax obligations and further detail will be set out in due course. Additionally, revenue raised from these reforms will be reinvested in improvements to the business rates system.

Reviving 'the beating heart of communities'

Speaking about the changes, Prime Minister Andy Burnham said: “For too long, governments have stood by while cherished venues have disappeared from our local high streets. So today I am changing that. This government will back the businesses that people want to see in their communities. I said I would protect pubs and local high streets – the beating heart of our communities – and that’s what we will do. What we’re announcing today is just the start as we work to bring back hope across the country.” Chancellor of the Exchequer John Healey said: “Pubs, clubs and live music venues are at the heart of communities across the UK. They help make a place what we love. They bring people together, support local jobs and help keep high streets and town centres busy — which is why we will back them all the way. We are determined to bring hope back, give businesses the support they need and generate growth in every postcode. This is just one step of the Government’s plans to back our communities and high streets and we will return to our commitment to reform the wider business rates system, including Small Business Rates Relief, at the Budget.”

'Breathing space'

Responding to the PM’s actions, Campaign for Real Ale (CAMRA) chairman Ash Corbett-Collins, said: “This is a brilliant start to the new Prime Minister’s tenure, living up to the statements that he’s made before about pubs, pints and people. Our business rates system is grossly unfair to pubs and social clubs, bricks and mortar businesses that are also vital social institutions for their communities and desperately needs reform. As always we need to see the details at the Budget, but another year of rate relief will provide vital breathing space to publicans in England, while the work to review the rating system for hospitality businesses presumably continues.” Independent brewers across England have also applauded Burnham in keeping his pub promise. Society of Independent Brewers and Associates (SIBA) chief executive Andy Slee added: “The new Prime Minister promised he would deliver much needed support for our cherished community pubs on business rates and he has acted quickly to do so. A 20% cut to business rates will make a real difference to the bottom lines. Our pubs and breweries contribute around 40% of turnover as taxation compared to online global giants that only pay a fraction of that to the Treasury. Rebalancing this tax burden on business rates away from businesses that contribute to the community and support jobs in our towns and villages will make a real difference to every postcode." Slee explained: "We hope this is the first step in wider support and that the Prime Minister also looks to reduce tax on draught beer sold in pubs and ensuring that small breweries can sell their beers to pubs across the UK.”

'Energy costs remain a huge worry'

UK business insurance firm Simply Business CEO, Julie Fisher, said that although she recognises that the announcement of a 20% cut to business rates pubs, clubs and live music venues is "a clear sign that the government is listening" and "seeing Prime Minister Andy Burnham willing to move quickly to relieve cost pressures on small businesses is certainly encouraging" but insisted that "the question that remains is whether this goes far enough, and how quickly independent operators will feel the benefit". Fisher pointed out that "energy costs remain a huge worry for hospitality businesses in particular. The latest ONS figures show the accommodation and food service activities industry had the highest proportion of businesses expressing concern about energy prices of any sector, at 88%. Against that backdrop, any reduction in fixed costs like business rates is welcome". Additionally, Fisher stated that while the announcement "won't solve that on its own, it’s a step in the right direction for SMEs who are trying so hard to survive" but added that "as the new Prime Minister develops his 10 year plan, we'd encourage the government to consider measures that would benefit all small businesses" and reiterated that "a thriving small business community would do wonders for our economy". Global Drinks founder and CEO Steve Perez, the company behind brands such as VK and Hooch, also warned that the move "won't make any material difference" unless it is followed by a VAT cut for the hospitality industry.

A cut in VAT is still needed

Perez explained: “Our business rates went up by 130% in April, so while this news of a 20% cut is of course very welcome, realistically it won’t make any material difference to any pub. It feels a bit like supermarkets putting up their prices only to then say later that they’ve reduced them." Perez said that “it’s also interesting to note that they haven’t mentioned restaurants and hotels, because of course most pubs these days serve food, while many also have accommodation. However for restaurants and hotels, not only do we have these higher business rates, we’ve also had increases of minimum wage, national insurance contributions and we’ve also had the introduction EPR, a new packaging tax". Perez added: “What hospitality is campaigning for right now is a cut in VAT. Ireland has just cut VAT to 9%, and Andy Burnham said prior to becoming prime minister that he would support a cut in VAT. That would make a huge difference for all of the hospitality industry. That would mean we can invest in our businesses, that would mean we can employ more people, and for the Government that would mean we would pay more corporation tax – which I think every business is happy to pay more tax if we make more money. But with these business rates currently, we have to pay before we even open the doors.”

title: Will crus unlock Oltrepò Pavese’s full potential?
summary:
Oltrepò Pavese is well-known as a stronghold of Italian Pinot Nero, particularly for traditional method sparkling wines. At Conte Vistarino, however, single-vineyard wines are demonstrating that the region’s prestige can also extend to still expressions.

The post Will crus unlock Oltrepò Pavese’s full potential? appeared first on The Drinks Business.
content:
Oltrepò Pavese is well-known as a stronghold of Italian Pinot Nero, particularly for traditional method sparkling wines. At Conte Vistarino, however, single-vineyard wines are demonstrating that the region’s prestige can also extend to still expressions. Even in Italy, where wine estates often measure their history in generations, Conte Vistarino can claim a remarkable legacy. In 1850, Count Augusto Giorgi di Vistarino introduced Pinot Nero to Oltrepò Pavese, importing the vines directly from Burgundy. Today, Pinot Nero is one of the region’s defining grapes. At the time, however, planting it among the hills of Rocca de’ Giorgi was a bold and farsighted decision. The French influence was also reflected in the estate’s approach to sparkling wine. Working alongside his friend Carlo Gancia, Augusto Giorgi di Vistarino helped pioneer the production of traditional method sparkling wines in Italy, producing the estate’s first in 1865. That vision established a tradition of Pinot Nero excellence that has endured for more than 175 years and is now carried forward by Ottavia Giorgi di Vistarino. “Our history is not something we simply preserve; it is a responsibility that encourages us to keep moving forward,” says Ottavia Giorgi di Vistarino. “Pinot Nero has shaped the identity of our family and our estate for generations. Today, our task is to reveal its most authentic expressions, allowing each vineyard to speak with its own voice.” Indeed, despite drawing on almost two centuries of experience, Conte Vistarino continues to explore new ways of expressing this famously demanding variety.

Maximising Pinot Nero’s potential

Pinot Nero is the cornerstone of Conte Vistarino’s production. Of the estate’s 102 hectares under vine, 65 are dedicated to the variety, which plays a leading role across both its sparkling and still wines. Yet the vineyards represent only a small part of the property. Extending across much of the municipality of Rocca de’ Giorgi, the estate covers 826ha, creating a largely unbroken natural environment around the vines. While many wine producers are now investing in rewilding and biodiversity, at Conte Vistarino they have long been part of the estate’s identity. Around 300ha are covered by woodland, providing a habitat for native wildlife including deer, wolves and pheasants. This ecological richness is complemented by a measured approach to farming: chemical fertilisers and intensive cultivation are avoided, with the emphasis placed on maintaining soil vitality and the long-term balance of the vineyards. These conditions support viticulture in general, but they are particularly valuable for Pinot Nero. Conte Vistarino’s vineyards lie across a series of hills shaped by streams and small rivers. The interplay of elevation, exposure and natural ventilation helps preserve freshness and aromatic precision, while the diversity of the sites allows the grape to express both delicacy and depth. The same care continues in the winery. A major redevelopment completed in 2017 introduced state-of-the-art technology, including a gravity-flow system designed to minimise intervention and protect the integrity of the fruit. Whether destined for a still or sparkling wine, the grapes are handled with the aim of preserving elegance, clarity and a sense of origin.

A site-by-site approach

Few grape varieties are as sensitive to terroir as Pinot Nero. Burgundy, its historic homeland and enduring benchmark, has built its reputation on the ability of the variety to translate even subtle differences between neighbouring vineyards into the glass. One of Conte Vistarino’s most significant developments has therefore been its commitment to site-specific winemaking across its crus. Led by Ottavia Giorgi di Vistarino, the cru project began by identifying the individual vineyards best suited to producing still Pinot Nero of distinctive character and longevity. Its success is now inspiring the estate to extend the same philosophy to its traditional-method sparkling wines. “The crus allow us to move beyond a general idea of Oltrepò Pavese and show the extraordinary diversity that exists within it,” says Lino Scaravonati, general manager of Conte Vistarino. “The region’s future reputation will depend on its ability to make this diversity clear and recognisable. Single-vineyard wines give us the language to communicate origin, identity and value, positioning Oltrepò Pavese among the world’s most compelling territories for Pinot Nero.” The sites share certain characteristics: hillside positions, complex soils and relatively small vineyard areas. They have also been planted with Burgundian clones selected for their ability to respond to the conditions of Rocca de’ Giorgi. Yet each cru reveals a markedly different personality. Costa del Nero covers six hectares close to the top of its hill. Its southern exposure encourages full ripening, producing a generous, fruit-driven Pinot Nero with subtle notes of oak. Tavernetto is a two-hectare vineyard situated at around 300 metres above sea level. Its protected position receives less rainfall than other parts of the estate, contributing to a wine with greater concentration, a fuller body and a distinctive spicy finish. Bertone, covering 1.57 hectares, is similarly sheltered but lies higher, at approximately 400 metres. Its elevation brings freshness and precision, while velvety tannins and fine acidity give the wine notable ageing potential. Pernice was the first of Conte Vistarino’s crus to be developed. Situated at around 250 metres and facing southeast, its soils combine calcareous clay, sand and silt. Despite the apparent harmony of its growing conditions, the resulting wine has an assertive, unconventional personality: energetic, forthright and unmistakably individual. Together, these wines make a persuasive case not only for the quality of Oltrepò Pavese, but also for its remarkable internal diversity. Their credentials have been reinforced over the years by consistent recognition from leading Italian and international critics that reflect not the success of a single vintage, but the growing maturity and consistency of Conte Vistarino’s crus. That reputation was further strengthened at this year’s Global Pinot Noir Masters, where all four wines received Silver medals – an impressive collective result that confirms the strength of the range and its ability to stand alongside internationally recognised expressions of Pinot Noir. This long-term vision also lies behind Réserve des Amis, Conte Vistarino’s most exclusive cru project. Conceived as a collection of carefully selected vintages of Pernice, Bertone and Tavernetto, each presented in a limited-edition case signed by Ottavia Giorgi di Vistarino, the project explores the ageing potential of the estate’s single-vineyard Pinot Nero wines. Through the dialogue between different crus and vintages, Réserve des Amis reveals how each site evolves over time while retaining its distinctive identity. It is both a celebration of the maturity achieved by the cru project and a statement of confidence in the longevity of Oltrepò Pavese Pinot Nero. For an estate with more than 175 years of history, these achievements are both a tribute to its legacy and evidence of its determination to evolve. If Oltrepò Pavese is to strengthen its international reputation as a great territory for Pinot Nero, its crus may well provide the clearest and most compelling path forward.
